data.title: Unusual Machines: From Vast Opportunity To Concrete Execution (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction: Upgrade from Hold to Strong Buy

In my June 2025 analysis of Unusual Machines (UMAC), I recognized the vast market demand for small drones against the backdrop of higher defense spending as well as the trend of using unmanned aerial vehicles for warfare. However, I merely gave a Hold rating since revenue had not been reflecting this trend and expenditures remained high, which makes me cautiously optimistic.

The latest earnings call showed that the Company is at a pivotal point, with revenue almost doubled at $4.9 million. Quoting the CEO's analogy: 2025 was loading the fuel in the rocket, but 2026 is the year of launch.

With the change of fundamentals, we are upgrading our thesis that, Unusual Machines has shown its execution quality and has shifted from a retail shop to an industrial powerhouse that has more than 80% of revenue from the B2B/B2G segment.

The regulatory moat

In December 2025, the Federal Communications Commission of the United States added the Uncrewed Aircraft Systems (UAS) and its critical components to its cover list, which aims to exert more control of technologies like drones to minimize the risks to national security. This prohibits drones and components that are produced outside the U.S. from being imported and operating in the country.

This arbitrary "moat" of domestic drone producers essentially removed major global competitors in both commercial and industrial drones from the U.S. market, like T-Motor and DJI. According to the earnings call, this created a "marketplace vacuum" in the market that the Company operates in, opening up "at least a $3 billion non-defense components marketplace" that domestic solutions will dominate within the next 3 to 5 years as older fleets retire.

What's more, in January 2026, the FCC updated its Blue UAS Cleared List, which aims to reinforce domestic supremacy by pushing drone products to have at least 65% of domestic components used. This has given UMAC a head start since multiple of its products are already in the framework, including the Rotor Riot Brave 55A ESC and Brave F7 flight controller.

According to the latest earnings results and how the CEO describes it, the regulatory shift drove its enterprise sales to more than 80% of the revenue mix from a mere 30% before the enforcement, because of its FCC/NDAA-compliant domestic supply chain. Gross margin also rose to 36% in Q4, as they are the largest domestic producer in several component categories, rising from 24% in Q1.

The $1 billion Pentagon backing

It has been obvious that this year we are in a "defense supercycle", driven mainly by the administration's pro-defense policies, actions, and, most importantly, spending. This provides a nice tailwind to UMAC, given also the Drone Dominance Program from the Department of War.

The program is worth $1.1 billion, funded by the "Big Beautiful Bill", aiming to rapidly develop and field low-cost, unmanned attack drones at scale. Given the importance of drones in modern warfare as exemplified in the Russia-Ukraine war and Iran war, the DoW plans to procure more than 300,000 small drones over the two years.

  • Gauntlet 1 (Feb–July 2026): 12 vendors selected to produce 30,000 units at an average price of $5,000 per drone.
  • Gauntlet 4 (Final Phase): Procurement increases to 150,000 units as the unit price target drops to $2,300.

For the record, more than half of the announced winners of Drone Dominance are already customers of ours in some form or fashion

- UMAC CEO Allan Evans

From the CEO of UMAC, of the 11 companies that qualified for the $150 million in delivery orders under Gauntlet 1, at least six are existing UMAC component customers. Therefore, this shows the importance of UMAC in support of this program, not as a competitor to the selected candidates, but as a necessary supplier. The average revenue potential of UMAC components in each drone under the program ranges from $500 (analyst estimates) to $1000 (management estimates), which translates into a $90 million opportunity in 2026 and $250 million in 2027.

Realizing the guided revenue requires operational readiness. To show that they can fulfill the demand, UMAC is in the transitional process to an automated motor production line in mid-2026, which will raise output volume to well over 100,000 monthly, as mentioned in the earnings call.

Financials: Recovery and significant growth in sight

The latest earnings present a bright picture of its future potential, from a piecemeal drone producer to a high-volume critical supplier of a technology that is of national security. Quarterly revenue reached $4.9 million, while total 2025 revenue totaled $11.2 million, both of which represent triple-digit growth driven by the pivot into enterprise sales that are supported by government spending - as enterprise sales % in the revenue mix increased from 31% in quarter 1 to 81% in quarter 4.

Given the discussed expansion in government spending, I estimate that the total revenue in 2026 can reach about $30 million (in line with consensus estimates) - which is one-third of the projected market from the DoW program, as the manufacturing capability of motor will reach 100,000 in the second half of 2026 and camera production will be online towards the end of the year.

More importantly, the Company is achieving the growth profitably. Its Q4 gross margin has shown improvement compared to earlier in the year. This is expected to continue in the next year once the scaling effect clicks in with the automated production.

A common worry for growth stock is its financial health. However, UMAC has shown an even more impressive performance in its management of capital and financing. The company closed 2025 with $157.4 million in the bank and no debt. This ensures the company is not constrained by resources to scale up in the face of higher demands.

Valuation

Amongst most small drone peers, UMAC is valued the lowest due to its size and the role in the drone supply chain (component-focused vs FPV / end-product). However, this latest earning shows that the company is no longer an "emerging player" for the future, but a mature supplier. As such, it is justifiable that the valuation multiple expand slightly from the current level. Therefore, the bull and bear case of valuation would be:

Bull Case Bear Case
Revenue ($m) 30 25
P/S 38x (10% from current level) 36x (5% from current level)
Target price (2026) $30 (50% upside) $24 (20% upside)

Investment risks

  • Execution over market tailwinds: The risk profile of the Company, as it matures and scales up, shifts from whether the market has demands for their products, to whether the management can scale up production fast enough and whether they can manage financials and expenditures well
  • Supply chain bottlenecks: The CEO has mentioned in the earnings call that the supply of key components like barometers has been constrained due to the sequential growth of the production and demand. With several wars happening in which drones are key, supply chain would be a key bottleneck
  • Operational integration: The risk of managing a workforce that grew nearly 10x in the past year and the technical challenge of the automated production make financial projection difficult to achieve

Conclusion

Unusual Machines has been considered a small, retail drone manufacturer and supplier in the past. However, with the backdrop of higher spending and focus on drones as a modern warfare, the Company has benefited and is playing a critical role in the industry. Together with the improving operational capability and solid financials, I believe it is time we upgrade the rating for the Company because of its concrete execution.

data.title: Dream Finders Homes: Valuation Not Yet Compelling Despite Stock Weakness
data.description:
Introduction

It’s been an uncertain last few months for the US home building industry. While the most recent new home sales have held up, margins and returns have been under pressure, and industry CEOs have been citing affordability constraints, elevated sales incentives, and consumer confidence as issues. And that’s all before the impact of the events in the Arabian Gulf, with gas prices rising and consumer sentiment likely to flounder further. The risk of political interference is a further complication, with even talk of stock buybacks being banned until affordability improves.

Dream Finders Homes (DFH) has not been shielded from these challenges, with recently reported 4q25 results demonstrating margin pressure and reduced return on equity. More positively, the order book is holding up, with new orders up 9% compared to 4q24.

In my view, the company’s strategy, discussed below, is perfectly legitimate. My enthusiasm for the stock is tempered, however, by what I believe is a somewhat unwarranted premium valuation, even after a sustained period of relative decline in the stock compared to other publicly traded peers. In the context of choppy sector conditions likely to persist for some time, this is not one of my preferred names, and so I rate the stock a hold.

A little background first

Dream Finders Homes was founded by CEO Patrick Zalupski in 2008 and came public in January 2021. The founder owns or controls around 61% of the shares as of early March this year. From the beginning as a public company, the company’s own press releases have emphasized that its business model revolves around being asset-light.

In its early years of being publicly-traded, the stock enjoyed a premium multiple of tangible book value compared to peers, as this comparison with D.R. Horton (DHI) and Taylor Morrison Home Corporation (TMHC) illustrates:

This is likely to have been because of relatively high returns on equity and frequent comparisons to fellow home builder NVR (NVR). This latter company has, of course, long pursued a capital-light model. This has enabled it to achieve sustained high returns and, arguably, to navigate industry downturns better than most. So, for a while, Dream Finders has historically looked like a ‘mini me’ of NVR, or at least the valuation arguably has implied as much.

However, there are significant differences between Dream Finders and NVR in terms of strategy, income statement performance, and capital structure, and I believe that this has been working against the company more recently. Most obviously, since April 2024, the stock has greatly underperformed both peers and the broader market, as can be seen from this comparison with a basket of larger and similarly sized peers:

As a result of this underperformance, it would be reasonable for investors to wonder whether this might be a buying opportunity because of some temporary factor that will soon reverse. However, in my opinion, the stock still trades at something of a premium, and to an extent that is not necessarily warranted given the financial history and outlook.

To see why, I think it’s helpful to break down the components of Dream Finders’ historic financial performance, discuss what the implications are, and then review the outlook in the context of current valuation compared to the peer group.

Copying NVR is hard

Let’s start with gross margin. Here is Dream Finders’ quarterly gross margin since IPO compared to NVR and a basket of similar-sized peers:

It’s also important to note at this point that the company arguably has a conservative accounting policy here, as it includes sales commission expense (which can typically be over 4% of sales) in cost of sales, unlike most peers, including the six on the chart. Leaving that aside, gross margin has gradually improved over the period.

So far, so good.

Next, EBIT margin:

Here there is a clear gap in performance versus both NVR and peers which has never really changed materially. Looking further under the hood, a major driver of this discrepancy may be SG&A costs as a percentage of sales:

NVR’s SG&A costs have ranged from 4.5% to 6.5%. Dream Finders’ equivalent margin has, in contrast, been between 7% and 10%, with a recent spike to over 11%. This performance is largely similar to the basket of peers, despite Dream Finders having the advantage of excluding selling commission in this line item. And so adjusting for that, differences in SG&A margin are likely to account for much of the relatively disappointing EBIT margin mentioned above.

There could, of course, be more than one reason for this differential performance at the EBIT margin level, with another possibility being a structurally above-average use of forward mortgage commitment programs, which are accounted for under SG&A. (Indeed, this topic is mentioned in passing in 4q25 earnings. If it is something like this, the explanation could be that the company’s business model demands rapid turnover, even if it means finding ways to shift product either through discounts, which hit revenues, or other sales techniques, which hit SG&A.

But in a sense, the cause doesn’t matter so much when it comes to my analysis. The fact is that by the EBIT line in the income statement, Dream Finders is unexceptional, at least when it comes to margins.

It's not all about margins

But wait! Of course, it’s not all just about percentage margins. A key part of the company’s strategy is to have high inventory turnover, a key driver of total asset turnover. Put very simply, with a faster moving business, the absolute dollar amount of profit that can be extracted from available capital in any given time period can be boosted. And the company itself is understandably proud of its success in this regard:

Investors should note, however, that while Dream Finders’ inventory turnover has ranged from 1.8x to 2.3x in the last few years, higher than most other builders, this is still substantially below that of NVR:

In my opinion, this is most likely to be the result of a greater degree of customization by Dream Finders compared to NVR, plus the latter company’s accumulated know-how and higher reliance on its own manufacturing facilities for certain standardized construction products. So in effect, while Dream Finders isn’t the same as most other builders on this metric, it has a long way to go to be truly a peer of high-multiple NVR.

Dream Finders isn’t NVR, but nor is it exactly like other builders

So pulling all these metrics together, what are the implications of having somewhat low EBIT margins and somewhat higher inventory turns and hence asset turnover? Well, it’s true that Dream Finders has relatively high return on equity compared to peers:

But when looking at return on total assets, to remove the effect of financial leverage, Dream Finders just doesn’t look that exceptional:

This, of course, demonstrates the ability of financial leverage to boost returns on equity. Here is the historic total debt to total capital for the comparison group (the company had $235m of cash at 31 December 2025 so net debt to equity is currently around 41%):

Put simply, the way the math works is that lower end EBIT margins and somewhat higher asset turnover net out to being a perfectly respectable return on assets, but not an outstanding one. In my view, higher financial leverage appears to be a significant contributor to the company’s upper-end return on equity. That’s a perfectly legitimate strategy, and I’m certainly not suggesting any issues with management quality. But potential owners of Dream Finders stock should be aware of what is driving the bottom line and apparent returns.

In a sense, I believe Dream Finders is most accurately thought of a something of a hybrid between the NVR model and a more traditionally-structured home builder. The question now is what would be a fair or attractive valuation to pay for this company given its business model and track record.

Despite share price weakness, the valuation still looks something of an outlier

In my view, it can be instructive to look at a home builder’s stock valuation in relation to its peers in several different contexts, notably returns on equity and relative financial leverage. This exercise can also shed light on the extent to which the market may be willing to pay a premium for larger, more diversified builders where geographical and end-market risk is more spread out.

I believe that, put simply, all else held equal, a home builder with a track record of higher returns on equity tends to (and indeed should) trade on a higher multiple of tangible book value. And this scatter plot showing the fourteen largest publicly traded builders (excluding NVR for clarity and Tri Point Homes owing to its recent takeover), is somewhat consistent with that:

In my view, one of the interesting nuances to emerge from this exercise is that the market appears to be willing to pay something of a multiple premium for one or two of the large-cap builders (notably D.R. Horton, Lennar). This, in my opinion, intuitively makes sense, given they are more diversified geographically and quite simply have more scale, meaning problems in one region or group of localities are unlikely to derail overall company performance by very much. There may also be some diversification benefits by end market, although that can vary (e.g., D.R. Horton has recently done well with a big focus on affordable homes for first-time buyers.)

The large-cap companies also generally have relatively low financial leverage, which is worth examining in its own right. Plotting a scatter chart of total debt compared to price-to-tangible book value ratio throws up some interesting results:

Overall, the market does appear to take the view that more indebted companies should be accorded a lower multiple to reflect the greater risk (and, to be fair, potentially greater reward in good times), while also being willing to award the large caps something of a premium to the fitted curve.

There are two notable outliers, however: Hovnanian and Dream Finders Homes. I believe that in the latter case, this is a significant anomaly, and it makes me cautious about the relative upside potential for Dream Finders compared to most publicly-traded builders.

For the avoidance of doubt, I don’t believe there are specific worries about the company’s debt maturity profile or refinancing risk. For example, it successfully raised $300m of senior notes in September 2025 at a 6.875% coupon, significantly down from a comparable round of debt financing in 2023 when the coupon was 8.25%.

Conclusion

Considering the company’s rapid growth and ascent up the industry rankings, it is certainly quite an achievement by the founder and his executive team to have gotten to where they are, and especially the growth since going public. But overall, when allowing for capital structure and some income statement metrics that aren’t so very different from peers, I don’t find the valuation compelling enough in the current environment and would look elsewhere for exposure to home building.

Why might I change my mind? Well, there are several aspects to this. My Hold rating is to an extent a sector-relative call, and anything that supports the home builder stocks in general would be a positive for Dream Finders Homes too.

This would include significantly lower interest rates (and hence mortgage rates), improved consumer confidence, a reduction in unemployment, and/or a pullback in certain commodity prices such as copper. Conversely, the opposite trends would obviously make me more bearish.

When it comes to stock specific events, evidence that Dream Finders Homes was achieving improved SG&A costs, net margins, or asset turnover that is even better than their already impressive levels (without a material margin hit) would be positive, and I’d re-examine my rating. As would the simple fact of further multiple compression, which could happen either by the stock staying at current levels and a continued growth in TBV or, alternatively, from a further decline in the equity. Assuming no deterioration in company execution, I would start to get very interested at around 1.1-1.2x TBV, the midpoint of which equates to a stock price of around $13. Investors should be aware that there is a 15% short interest in the stock, and so there is material scope for a short squeeze should sentiment shift positively.

data.title: McDonald's: The Big Arch And Discount Meals, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
McDonald’s (MCD) has been making itself a big deal of late.

The burger chain recently released its new Big Arch sandwich using an innovative social media pitch involving CEO, Chris Kempczinski. While initially panned across social media, the saying of “any publicity is good publicity” came to full fruition as other CEOs elsewhere, including Tom Curtis of Burger King, joined in on the fun.

According to the Wall Street Journal ("WSJ"), MCD is also preparing to roll out a new value menu aimed at budget-conscious consumers. This would include meals for $3 or less and a new $4 breakfast meal. I believe this initiative could help sustain MCD as a continued outperformer in a period of more uncertain economic conditions.

Shares are already having a good run in the face of heightened volatility elsewhere. The stock is up 6% over the past year, most of which has come on a YTD basis.

At current trading levels, I view the outlook as positive and see shares as fairly valued.

MCD Stock Key Metrics

MCD currently commands a forward trading multiple of about 25x forward earnings. This is viewed negatively by the Seeking Alpha (“SA”) quants, and the poor scoring on valuation is ultimately one of the main factors driving the ‘hold’ rating from the SA quant lens.

This is despite positive marks on both profitability and momentum. While I don’t view the multiple as cheap, I see it as reasonably priced for a restaurant chain that continues to roll out new growth initiatives timely with the current economic environment.

While the broader SA community is also largely neutral on MCD’s prospects, many on Wall Street are more bullish.

That said, the community appears to be trending to the neutral category, with 14 analysts over the last 90 days rating shares as a ‘hold’. In addition, consensus price targets on the stock see just 5.5% upside from current levels.

I certainly believe MCD can attain this mark, especially with its new initiatives under way, though I don’t find the potential particularly attractive for new investors seeking positioning into the stock.

MCD Operating Performance

In its most recent release for Q4, I believe that MCD showed investors that its operating model continues to hold up well even in what Kempczinski described as a challenging quick-service restaurant environment.

For the full year, the company reported 5.5% YOY growth in constant currency terms, while comparable sales increased more than 3%. This was aided in part by a 5.7% acceleration in Q4 alone.

One standout for me was that growth was driven not only by higher ticket sizes but also by positive guest traffic. In my view, this is key sign that the brand continues to resonate with consumers.

Within the U.S., results were particularly strong, with 6.8% comparable sales growth in Q4, supported by a combination of marketing campaigns, menu innovation, and renewed emphasis on value offerings.

The management team also highlighted widening guest-count advantages versus competitors during the quarter. To me, this suggests that MCD is continuing to capture market share within the QSR space.

International markets also performed well, with comparable sales rising 5.2% across the company’s major operating markets, including solid results in the U.K., Germany, and Australia. Meanwhile, developmental license markets such as Japan and China continued expanding the global footprint.

Looking ahead, management plans to accelerate restaurant expansion, targeting 2,600 new openings in 2026 as the company works toward its long-term goal of 50K locations by 2027.

MCD’s Big Arch And Value Meals

Perhaps the biggest headline-grabber for MCD recently has been the release of its new Big Arch burger, a hefty sandwich built with two quarter-pound beef patties, three slices of white cheddar, crispy and slivered onions, and a new tangy “Big Arch Sauce.”

The burger has already launched across several international markets and has even secured a permanent place on the menu in the U.K., suggesting early traction with consumers.

Initial reviews have largely described the sandwich as flavorful and satisfying, if a bit messy. From a business sales perspective, analysts expect the launch to drive a meaningful bump in traffic during the early months of its U.S. rollout.

Ironically, much of the attention surrounding the burger came from a viral moment involving Kempczinski. A taste-test video posted to social media quickly circulated online and drew widespread panning, even prompting rival chains like Burger King to join the conversation.

While the clip may have drawn laughs, it ultimately generated millions of views and significant publicity for the product. This, of course, turned into sales, which MCD has noted is already exceeding expectations. This should bode well for MCD heading into its Q1 release early in May.

At the same time, the company appears to be doubling down on affordability. According to the WSJ’s reporting, the management team is preparing to introduce a refreshed value push that includes items priced at $3 or less and a new $4 breakfast meal deal.

In my view, pairing menu innovation with aggressive value offerings positions MCD's well to attract both higher-income diners and more budget-conscious consumers.

Is MCD Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

In my view, MCD continues to demonstrate strong operating performance, steady traffic growth, and the ability to capture market share even when industry conditions are less than ideal.

I also believe that initiatives such as the Big Arch launch and the upcoming refresh of the value menu also show that the management team is actively working to keep the brand culturally relevant while addressing the needs of more price-sensitive consumers.

I do believe that the stock at current levels already reflects much of this competitive value advantage. Trading at roughly 25x forward earnings, I don’t find shares in MCD to be a value discount, especially with shares trading near the top-end of its 52-week range.

While the company’s consistent profitability and leadership position justify a premium multiple, I believe the current multiple leaves somewhat limited room for outsized upside in the near term.

That said, I still view the outlook as positive. Continued restaurant expansion, steady digital engagement through the loyalty ecosystem, and continued menu innovation should allow the company to maintain its leadership position in the global QSR market.

For existing shareholders, these factors provide several reasons to remain invested, in my view.

On the other hand, given the relatively modest upside implied by consensus price targets and the current valuation, I believe MCD is best kept as a solid ‘hold’ at present levels for new investors, with the potential to become more attractive on any meaningful pullback.

data.title: Philip Morris: I Will Wait For A Deeper Pullback Before Adding More (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Philip Morris (PM) is one of my key holdings in the consumer staples portfolio. I think it's uniquely positioned to capitalize on the current market trends in the tobacco industry, namely the smoke-free movement. In fact, I consider PM a leader in this area.

I've been a shareholder for quite some time, but my first bullish article about PM was published in July 2024, when I argued for its more attractive risk-to-reward profile when compared to peers. Since then, the stock price has increased by nearly 53%, outperforming the broad market by quite a lot. My investment from that time has generated me nearly 62% total returns when I account for dividends.

I've been bullish in my last article on PM as well. Everything was going right, but I see that the market is shifting its outlook on this business. Over the course of last month, I've seen PM's stock price decline by over 10%. I think the change in dynamic is a result of PM's last earnings release for Q4 2025 and relatively high multiple when compared to the likes of Altria Group (MO).

That's why I decided to prepare follow-up coverage and present you with my thoughts on PM's business, financials, and valuation. To say right from the get-go, I am currently holding onto my shares, and I'm not adding at this moment. I consider PM a Hold, in fact a key Hold in my portfolio, but let me explain why I believe so.

The Business Looks As Good As Ever

High-level numbers show strong performance

I'm sorry to those who weren't satisfied with PM's results, but I don't view it that way. I'm actually impressed with PM's continuous progress, which I see resulted in 1.4% organic volume growth. As I see PM noted in its Investor Presentation, that's a fifth year in a row of a positive volume performance. With PM's strong pricing power, I was happy to see 6.5% revenue growth.

That's not the end of positive news, as I'm even more satisfied with PM's profitability. Just look at the summary I pasted below: double-digit growth in terms of both operating income margin and diluted EPS. From my perspective, it's nothing short of a strong performance, especially as I treat it as an indication of improving margins.

Here's how the management opened the last Earnings Call. I'm pasting their high-level take on 2025 below:

2025 was another outstanding year for PMI. The shift of adult smokers to better alternatives is the lasting structural movement, one that we continue to lead and from which we are generating strong sustained growth. Our leading global position in smoke-free products enables us to deliver a fifth consecutive year of positive volumes with a rapid top line progress and significant margin expansion.

PM continues its leadership in the smoke-free movement

I've always said: PM is the leader of the smoke-free movement. I see that in 2025, the share of the smoke-free segment in PM's results amounted to as much as 41.5%. I'd like to remind you that PM is active in markets that are much less susceptible to this trend than, for example, the U.S.

Despite that, MO has a much more modest share of smoke-free portfolio in its top line. The last time I covered MO, I saw it amounted to ~12.5%. That's over three times less than PM despite the fact that, as I mentioned above, U.S. consumers are more likely (in general) to seek such alternatives to smokeable products.

I'd like to highlight that smoke-free growth is not just about the top line. As I see in the charts I shared above, smoke-free products now generate ~42.9% of PM's gross profit. I also realize that this segment is the future of PM. Just look at the chart I pasted below. Despite a solid and quite resilient performance of cigarette volume, I see a general downward trend. In 2025, cigarette volume has decreased by 1.5%, but I was happy to see that SFP saved the day by growing its volume by as much as 12.8%.

The outlook for realizing PM's targets for the 2024-2026 period is very optimistic

I remember that a couple of years ago PM shared a few targets for 2024-2026. Now, with two-thirds of that period already behind us, I'm very happy to see that PM is on the path to not only meet but also outperform its goals.

I think the organic shipment volume is likely to turn out positive, and net revenue growth will likely reach the higher end of the primary range (6-8%). I think the true gift for shareholders lies in profitability, as both operating income and diluted EPS are on the path to outperform PM's previous targets.

I heard the management discuss it during the Earnings Call. I'm pasting their outlook on that below:

Indeed, we have successfully achieved our 3-year CAGR targets for organic OI and currency-neutral EPS in 2 years. With another strong performance expected in 2026, despite some transitory headwinds, we are today renewing these growth targets for the next 3 years, further validating our best-in-class growth profile within the consumer packaged goods.

Importantly, this is accompanied by strong and increasing cash generation, and we target a leverage ratio of close to 2x by the end of 2026 at prevailing exchange rates. With our dividend payout now close to our objective of around 75% of adjusted diluted EPS, this provides capacity for strong returns to shareholders.

It's Not All Sunshine And Rainbows

PM has just taken a hit from India

I don't think this will come as any surprise or news for PM's shareholders, but PM's been trying to enter India with its smoke-free portfolio, especially IQOS. India has a large population of smokers, so I think that it could be a win-win scenario with consumers getting less risky alternatives and PM expanding its business. But I don't see it happening in the foreseeable future given the note I'm pasting below:

Philip Morris International is on watch after India confirmed that it will not relax its nationwide ban on e-cigarettes and heated tobacco products. The decision is a clear setback to Philip Morris International's (PM) efforts to launch IQOS and similar devices in the nation. The health ministry stated it is not considering amending or easing the 2019 law, which explicitly includes heat-not-burn products, and reiterated its commitment to evidence-based tobacco control and cessation policies.

(...)

India’s decision keeps in place one of the world’s strictest stances on newer nicotine products in a market that sells over 100 billion conventional cigarettes annually. For Philip Morris, the decision closes off a large potential IQOS market for now and underscores regulatory risk around its overarching “smoke-free future” strategy.

The valuation is not an opportunity

I'm a shareholder of both PM and MO. I own both for different reasons, but if I were to choose one, I would go with PM. I think it's better positioned to generate solid shareholder returns in the long-term given its much higher progress on the smoke-free front.

But I only say that given the fact that I already own a meaningful stake of shares in PM. Given the pushback in India and the valuation gap between PM and MO, I don't think it's an attractive addition at the moment. Don't get me wrong, I think PM deserves to trade at a premium to MO, but the gap seems to be getting less justified.

Just look at the chart above. I see that PM trades at nearly 20x forward earnings while MO trades below 12x. India would be a substantial victory for PM, and it could justify such an extensive premium, but at this point I'm not confident in PM's valuation.

I think the market has a similar outlook, and that's why we've been watching a downward pressure on PM's stock price since February.

Final Words

I think you already know that I have a deep respect for PM's team and its business. I'm very satisfied with their results in 2025: positive volume growth, increasing progress on the smoke-free movement, and solid margin improvements.

At the same time, I can't walk past the fact that I counted on PM's victory in India and capitalization of the IQOS opportunity there. I'm not surprised with a downward pressure on PM's stock price given recent developments on that front. Also, I feel that investors are concerned with the large valuation premium PM holds over MO, which, in my view, limits the upside potential.

That's why I'm not buying more shares at the moment. I'm not selling any of those I've gathered over the years. I simply put PM on Hold and intend to collect dividends and reinvest them elsewhere. I will update you if my thesis changes, but I'd like to see a more attractive price or a meaningful business victory (comparable to what India could be) to start buying again. For now, PM is a Hold for me.

data.title: Allspring Intermediate Tax/AMT-Free Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
GENERAL FUND INFORMATION

Ticker: WITIX

Portfolio managers:

Robert Miller;

Bruce Johns;

Adrian Van Poppel;

and Nicholos Venditti, CFA®

Subadvisor: Allspring Global Investments, LLC

Category: Muni national interm

FUND STRATEGY

  • Uses both bottom-up credit research and top-down macroeconomic analysis

Quarterly review

  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal 1–15 Year Blend Index, for the three-month period that ended December 31, 2025.
  • Duration, credit, and selection were drivers of performance while sector allocation detracted.

Market review

Municipal bonds had a strong fourth quarter, posting a positive return of 1.56%. This outpaced most taxable fixed income asset classes. Only U.S. mortgage-backed securities (MBS) delivered a stronger fourth quarter return at 1.71%. Outside of MBS and tax-exempt municipals, the next best-performing fixed income asset classes were taxable municipals (1.37%) and U.S. commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS, 1.34%). While still positive, the 10-year Treasury and U.S. corporates had the lowest fixed income returns for the quarter at 0.83% and 0.84%, respectively.

Within municipal credit tiers, BBB-rated bonds performed the best for the quarter, returning 1.77%. Interestingly, high yield municipal debt was the worst-performing municipal credit sector, though it still posted a positive return of 1.15%. For the year, returns were similar across all investment-grade municipal debt, with AAA- through BBB-rated bonds returning between 4.35% and 4.13%. The outlier for the year was high yield municipal debt, which returned just 2.46%. The high yield municipal sector was weighed down by a high-profile credit that experienced a technical default and was downgraded multiple notches. Despite this underperformance, municipal high yield spreads remained rich relative to historical averages.

The municipal AAA yield curve flattened further in the fourth quarter, continuing the trend that began in September. While most of the curve flattening in the quarter happened in the first three weeks of October, the combined September/October flattening move was enough to completely unwind the steepening that took place in the first eight months of the year. The AAA municipal 2-year/10-year yield curve did move marginally steeper in November and December to finish the year very close to where it started.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) lowered its benchmark interest rate at both its October and December meetings. This combined 50-basis-point (bp; 100 bps equal 1.00%) decrease left the overnight federal funds rate at 3.50–3.75%. With most estimates of the neutral rate somewhere between 2.75% and 3.25%, the market is pricing in just over two more cuts in 2026, which would bring the benchmark rate close to neutral. This bias for lower rates, combined with a more dovish FOMC chair coming in May, leads us to expect the yield curve to steepen again in 2026.

Fund attribution and strategy

Duration—modest detractor

  • While we were short duration as the market rallied in the fourth quarter, overweights in the 6- to 8-year and 10- to 15-year duration buckets were positive, as these partial duration buckets outperformed.
  • Municipal bond returns were positive for the quarter. October municipal performance was particularly strong, especially given the typical weakness we usually see during the large primary issuance calendar in October. While still positive, November and December returns were more subdued, as some of the October performance pulled forward gains from year-end.
  • As we head into 2026, we are biased toward a shorter duration posture. We are expecting another year of strong inflows, offset by another year of record primary issuance. We believe that attractive valuations relative to other fixed income asset classes, along with still-compelling tax-exempt yields, should drive another year of solid performance. Like 2025, we believe income will be the main driver of returns, as rate volatility could increase with a new FOMC chair.

Yield curve—modest contributor

  • Underweights to bonds inside of eight years was positive as this maturity range underperformed. Overweights to the 8- to 12-year ranges were positive and offset an underweight to the 12- to 17-year range as these maturity ranges outperformed.
  • The municipal benchmark curve saw an “operation twist” rewind in October. The front end of the yield curve moved higher in yield, while the long end continued the September rally. The 1-year/30-year AAA municipal curve ended the third quarter at 192 bps and flattened to a low of 153 bps in November. We did see the curve steepen again in December, to close the year at 165 bps.
  • We believe the increase in front-end municipal yields was more technical in nature (accounts were overweight and the sector had become rich), and the new FOMC chair will be supportive for front-end rates (low likelihood of an increase in overnight rates). For these reasons, we think the bias will be for the yield curve to steepen again in 2026, continuing the trend that started in December.

Quality—contributor

  • An overweight to BBB rated bonds offset underweights to AAA and AA rated bonds, as all three rating categories outperformed. An overweight to A rated bonds was a modest detractor, as this rating category underperformed.
  • Credit spreads remain tight. Despite the rate volatility, the spread between AAA and high yield credits remains near the tightest levels over the past two years, making credit relatively more expensive. Looking ahead, we believe credit fundamentals are well supported by the economy, though the possibility of credit spread widening remains ever present. There are opportunities for more risk-taking investors to explore the high yield space; however, as the recent downgrade of Brightline demonstrates, security selection remains crucial in this segment of the market.

Sector—detractor

  • We continue to be underweight general obligation (GO) bonds and overweight revenue bonds. Controlling for duration, curve, and quality allocation, contributors to relative performance included an overweight allocation to the housing, hospital, education, and special tax sectors as well as underweight allocations to the prerefunded and state GO sectors. Detractors included an overweight allocation to the industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue sector.
  • In times of economic uncertainty, GO bonds backed by strong income and sales tax receipts, along with bonds supported by essential services provided by municipalities, are generally more defensive than discretionary sectors. We remain focused on these areas while also seeking opportunities in higher-beta revenue sectors where price dislocations create value. These dynamics underscore the need for an experienced team with institutional bandwidth to navigate an evolving market.

Issue selection—detractor

  • Controlling for duration, curve, quality, and sector allocation effects, security selection was negative for the quarter. Relative to the benchmark, the largest contribution effects were seen in the electric, hospital, and education sectors. Selection within the state GO, housing, and leasing sectors was negative.
  • The volatility observed throughout the year serves as a reminder to bond investors that municipals are credit products and not just a rates asset class. Security selection remains critical, as certain credits may diverge from the broader market due to forced selling, liquidity constraints, and structural factors. These inefficiencies can lead to atypical execution and present opportunities for strong risk-adjusted returns among liquidity providers. Prudent liquidity management has been a hallmark of our investment process, enabling us to capitalize on ongoing market dislocations while identifying compelling relative value opportunities.

data.title: Netskope: Look Past The Lockup Expiration As Company Turns FCF Positive
data.description:
In the midst of an already volatile trading year in 2026, the last few weeks in the stock market have turned decidedly more bearish as investors weighed the potential impacts from an extended conflict in the Middle East. Tech shares that were already being hammered due to the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative continued to sell off further, despite strong Q4 results.

Netskope (NTSK), the AI-focused cybersecurity platform, is one of the most prominent recent victims. Despite reporting healthy Q4 results that featured healthy retention rates and improving margins that tilted full-year free cash flow positive, the stock sold off more than 15% post-earnings, interrupting a rebound rally that had taken the stock off February lows around $9 (which was when Claude released cybersecurity features that spooked the sector).

I last wrote a buy article on Netskope in February, when the stock was trading at YTD lows in the mid-$9s. Since then, the company's Q4 earnings print has only deepened my conviction in the company. Netskope is amply proving that companies are feeling the need to reinvest in their cybersecurity amid the shift to running AI applications and agents, which open up new vulnerability points. The company continues to showcase excellent growth at scale while also maturing into an FCF-positive company.

One of the main reasons shares of Netskope sold off is that Netskope's earnings directly preceded the stock's lockup expiration, which expires on March 13. This is the date at which insiders, who were barred from selling post-IPO for a 180-day window, will be able to unload their shares. But recall that Netskope went public at $19 per share. After the post-earnings selloff, the stock is hovering closer to $10: nearly half of the IPO price. Netskope's price provides a self-regulating mechanism here: there aren't likely going to be many insiders who want to dump shares while it's priced irrationally low. With this in mind, I'm ignoring the post-earnings selling pressure and reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

What investors should focus on instead is the fact that Netskope issued a fantastic outlook for FY27, while the continued selloff has made the stock look even cheaper. At current post-earnings share prices near $10, Netskope trades at a market cap of $3.93 billion. After we net off the $1.16 billion of cash and $721.0 million of convertible debt on Netskope's most recent balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $3.49 billion.

As shown in the chart above, the company is guiding to $870-$876 million in revenue this year, or 23% y/y growth, which is above the $864 million (+22% y/y) that Wall Street had expected. It's also continuing to make gradual improvements to its profit profile (which is admittedly the weak link in the Netskope story), with pro forma operating margins expected to improve 6 points y/y to -10%.

Netskope trades at 4.0x EV/FY27 revenue. Before the SaaSpocalypse carved billions out of software stock valuations, a ~4x multiple was typically reserved for penalty-box software stocks, those growing under ~10% per year; while companies with mid-20s growth frequently traded at double-digit revenue multiples.

And as shown in the chart above, even though Netskope is hardly the only cyber stock shouldering deep selloff pain, its valuation sits well below most peers (despite trading closer in-line last year).

Not only is Netskope cheaper than many cybersecurity peers, but it has a well-entrenched and differentiated moat as well. Netskope is one of the earlier cybersecurity companies to introduce GenAI-specific security products. The rise of agentic AI, which has access to corporate data and is authorized to perform specific actions, requires purpose-built guardrails and visibility tools. The company's ability to sustain >30% revenue growth despite hitting a near-$1 billion annualized revenue scale provides concrete evidence that AI is a true tailwind for the company.

All in all, I continue to view Netskope as a deeply oversold stock, and the lockup expiration isn't likely to trigger a wave of insider selling. Buy the dip here and stay long.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Netskope's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Netskope's revenue in Q4 grew 32% y/y to $196.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $189.1 million (+27% y/y) by a meaningful five-point margin. Growth also barely decelerated from 33% y/y growth in Q3.

Meanwhile, the company's ARR also continues to build. In Q4, the company added $57 million of net-new ARR within the quarter, growing 31% y/y to $811 million. I think it's also worth recognizing that Netskope's current ARR already covers 93% of the company's guidance target for FY27, giving the company a very high degree of revenue visibility. RPO, or essentially contracted backlog, also grew 36% y/y to $1.2 billion.

We note as well that Netskope's net revenue retention rates hit 116% in the full year FY26, which is three points stronger than 113% in FY25. What this means is that the average Netskope customer is spending 16% y/y more on the Netskope platform relative to the previous year, net of customers who churn. A growing number of customers are adopting 5+ or 6+ products as part of their subscription bundle.

All of these customer metrics are very telling indicators that the company isn't suffering any near-term, AI-driven exodus of business. The >30% y/y growth in longer-term RPO, in particular, is a good indicator that customers are building on the Netskope platform for the long haul.

The one risk that Netskope faces is that it's still in the earlier days of achieving profitability, as it focuses on sales growth. Pro forma operating margins in Q4 hit -10%, improving 5 points sequentially and y/y. The company's guidance for FY27 implies 6 points of full-year improvement next year: though Netskope is still a long way from full profitability and from hitting a "Rule of 40" profile.

That said, Netskope did eke out a positive FCF margin in FY26, generating $12.4 million of FCF at a 2% margin. The company is expecting FCF margins to improve further to a 2-4% margin range in FY27.

Risks and key takeaways ey takeaways

Netskope delivered a terrific fourth-quarter earnings print and robust guidance outlook for FY27, with the stock mostly falling on insider selling fears after the lockup expiration. That said, with valuations so low, we think the stock is deeply oversold (and won't trigger that much selling pressure even when insiders are allowed to release their shares).

Of course, there are risks to keep in mind here. As I previously mentioned, Netskope doesn't score tremendously well from a "Rule of 40" perspective. It's still a long way away from breakeven operating margins. There's also competition to consider, as cloud security companies like Zscaler (ZS) also release their own versions of agentic AI security products.

That said, I do think Netskope's low valuation helps to offset and price in many of these risks. Given strong growth, healthy ARR and RPO trends, and the company's ongoing cash flow on top of a cash-rich balance sheet, Netskope is well set up for a rebound.

data.title: TSS Inc: Scaling Fast, But Not Yet Converting
data.description:
Investment thesis

TSS, Inc (TSSI) is a microcap IT/AI infrastructure company. That it can generate revenue is already proven. It exited 2025 with strong operating momentum. However, its business model is procurement-heavy and its customer base is highly concentrated. Therefore, TSSI's story is whether it can convert that scale into sustained margin expansion. This will become clearer as they report earnings in the next few quarters. Until then, I rate TSSI a Hold with upside bias.

Business Overview – Rapid Scale in AI Infrastructure Integration

Procurement, at 80%, is TSSI's largest revenue driver. This is a lower margin business that sources and delivers hardware at scale for the AI industry.

Interestingly, higher margin segments like systems integration and facilities services represent 42% of gross profits in 9M 2025 despite being only 16% of revenue.

A little calculation tells us that every dollar of Systems Integration / Facilities revenue produces ~4 times more profit than every dollar of Procurement revenue. A quick sensitivity check: suppose SI+FM rises from 16% of revenue mix to just 25% - a modest 9% growth. This causes a ~17.6% improvement in gross profit. Notably, that math does not need any revenue growth at all - even a modest mix shift will expand earnings disproportionately.

The market isn’t pricing that because the reverse is also true. If Procurement increases and SI/FM fall, revenue can grow while gross profit barely moves. Right now, TSSI remains a low-margin, procurement-heavy business and the market is still looking for signals of mix improvement before it rerates.

There has been tremendous expansion in operational scale. Power capacity went from 2.7MW to 15MW, a ~5x increase, by Q3. Operational footprint has also doubled to ~213,000 square feet. Production at the new Georgetown facility began and has become fully operational by June 2025.

This isn’t a small expansion increment - this is substantial build-out in anticipation of higher rack throughput. So operating leverage becomes embedded now: fixed costs are in place ahead of the full ramp in rack integration throughput, which only began to show up meaningfully in Q4 2025 results. So either they are built to scale, or they are overbuilt - there is not much middle ground.

So they have larger capacity, more power, higher utility and depreciation, increased staffing. Those are the front-loaded costs. Now if rack volume ramps up, operating leverage kicks in, earnings efficiency increases. If volume stalls, EBITDA goes down fast. That’s the risk.

In order to address/mitigate that, TSSI signed a multi-year agreement with its primary customer. This is supposed to cover variable and fixed costs associated with AI rack integration activities. The agreement does, in theory, improve baseline revenue visibility while also reducing cost exposure.

Now, the agreement says there’s a minimum monthly volume that is “expected. There are also fixed monthly payments. Together, they should cover facility costs, as well as interest payments on the debt. The agreement talks about a minimum weekly rack quantity, but is not explicit about a margin floor. It can also be terminated by the customer, subject to some fixed cost payments that will continue. However, the minimum volume commitment will cease.

That means, the agreement - if it stands - will utilize the buildout and cover some costs. However, the whole thing is not fixed in stone. It can be annulled. Moreover, TSSI is still financially responsible for fixed and variable costs associated with the facility.

Financial Snapshot – Strong Growth, Improving Balance Sheet

TSSI released full year and Q4 earnings, where we note that 2025 was a record year for the company, with full-year revenue increasing 66% to $245.7 million, up from $148 million in 2024. That is high growth. In FY2024, there was “hyper-growth” at 172% - which is to be attributed to a much smaller base year (2023) and initial ramp. So the question isn’t why growth fell last year, but whether the high 2025 growth is sustainable.

The one major variable is procurement. In Q3, we saw that revenue declined 40% yoy due to reduced procurement revenue. Management has admitted that procurement can “fluctuate wildly” depending on deal timing and federal buying cycles.

For full year 2025, adjusted EBITDA reached $18.6 million, up 83% from $10.2 million the previous year and above the company’s prior guidance range. Full year gross margin was down 2 percentage points from 2024, largely because of the Georgetown facility, and procurement mix. That means, revenue is expanding at a higher rate than margin.

What is uncontroverted is that the balance sheet has materially strengthened. TSSI ended 2025 with $85.5 million in unrestricted cash and cash equivalents, up more than $62 million from the prior year. Net working capital improved to $46.1 million from just $1.3 million at the end of 2024. Thus, the balance sheet is now much stronger than before. They have already deployed the entire liquidity required to build the Georgetown facility, so liquidity is no longer a concern.

Market Performance and Sentiment – Growth vs. Skepticism

Despite revenue growth and a new facility, the stock has done poorly. Shares are down substantially from the peak of the past year. Market cap, at roughly $318 million, is only about ~1.3× full-year 2025 revenue of $245.7 million. The market appears to be deeply skeptical, refusing to price TSSI as a high growth software business. Instead, it is rating TSSI as a low-margin infrastructure business - even then, the multiple is really compressed.

In short, the market is looking at TSSI as a company burdened by procurement economics. Unless TSSI shifts meaningfully towards higher-margin revenues, or demonstrates high operating leverage, valuation seems to be anchored in low-margin assumptions.

The Bull Case

Bulls assume or say three things - a) that AI infrastructure demand remains structurally strong, b) that the current valuation misprices TSSI by basing itself on procurement-heavy economics rather than on potential margin expansion, and c) that balance sheet risk has largely been removed.

First, the bull case says that TSS’s business is in the high-growth AI infra deployment. The high-growth nature shows in the sustained revenue growth in the last two years. Potential for improvement in AI-enabled rack integration activity has led the company to expand capacity build-out. This infrastructure, now fully operational, has begun to see meaningful rack throughput ramp, with further volume growth expected.

Second, following from that first argument, the bull case says valuation is mispricing TSSI. TSSI stock is now priced for low-margin procurement, even though SI+FM is improving in the mix, already generating ~4x more gross profit per revenue dollar than Procurement. Even a modest mix improvement will drastically improve profitability. This does not even require proportionate revenue growth, just a “simple” shift of revenue mix.

Third, the positive view points to the substantial amelioration in liquidity risk. Balance sheet strength has substantially improved, net working capital has been strengthened. Buildout capital is already deployed, so TSSI is no longer capital-constrained. They are simply waiting for execution.

The Bear Case

The bear case also says three things. It argues that procurement is still a big driver of TSSI’s revenues. It claims lack of margin expansion despite some improvement in scale. Lastly, it is concerned about the dependence on one primary customer for buildout utilization creates downside risk.

First, as to procurement, bears point out that it still comprises 80% of revenue. Management has said it can fluctuate widely. It has an inordinate effect on revenue, viz in Q3, revenue declined 40% yoy just because procurement declined in that quarter. If this continues to be the situation, and higher-margin business never dominates, then, despite improvement in scale, gross profit and EBITDA will remain inconsistent. In that scenario, scale does not translate into predictability.

The second bear argument is that consolidated gross margin has declined in 2025 over 2024. Although there is improvement in Q4, bears argue that a single good quarter does not negate the trend. Unless margins expand meaningfully, the fixed cost buildout will not generate operating leverage. The embedded convexity works both ways.

Third, per TSSI’s own claims, they are heavily dependent on one prime customer with whom they have a multi-year agreement. This agreement includes provisions that would help utilize front-loaded buildout. However, the structure of the agreement only mentions a minimum volume, and that too is conditional. If volume does not materialize, if there’s decline in volume from its largest OEM customer, this will materially impact results.

That is why the bears argue that the stock deserves to trade at procurement-weighted multiples rather than rerating on margin convexity.

Judgment

Both arguments have merit, and they can both be reconciled by understanding that TSSI is a company in transition.

On the one hand, even bears cannot doubt that its revenue growth is real. Its infrastructure build-out is real, and there is no doubt that the balance sheet has materially strengthened. The multi-year agreement provides revenue visibility. If these things hold true, operational collapse is not a practicality.

On the other hand, even bulls cannot argue that a weak gross margin is a good thing, or that high procurement in the revenue mix is laudable. The improvement in EBITDA seen in Q4 must now prove sustainable over the next several quarters.

Between these two opposing views stands only one variable - execution. Can the company convert scale into expanded margin? That is the key unknown.

Given that mix shift and ensuing margin improvement has not yet shown up on reported results, but that infrastructure and liquidity risks have declined, the most defensible position is a Hold with upside bias.

Morningstar Rating™

Overall Rating as of December 31, 2025

★★★★

(4-Star) Class Y

★★★★

As of 12/31/2025 the fund’s Class A and Class Y shares received a 4 star overall Morningstar rating™, measuring risk- adjusted returns against 1004, 936 and 755 Large Growth funds over the 3-, 5- and 10- year periods, respectively. A fund’s overall rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) rating metrics.

Key Takeaways

  • Markets: US equity markets delivered gains in the fourth quarter, driven by strong earnings and Fed interest-rate cuts. Gains were modest for large-cap growth stocks.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in health care, industrials, and financials
  • Detractors: Stock selection in communication services and information technology
  • Outlook: We continue to see structural tailwinds and exciting multiyear trends that we believe can drive sustained growth for many businesses.

Performance Review

  • The fund (class Y)(PGOYX) posted positive absolute returns but lagged its benchmark for the quarter. Stock selection in communication services and information technology weighed on relative returns, as did an overweight position in real estate (less than 2%). The relative weakness was offset by holdings in health care, industrials, and financials.
  • Top contributors to relative performance included an overweight position in Eli Lilly (LLY)(health care) and an underweight position in Oracle (ORCL) (information technology). Top detractors included an underweight position in Alphabet (GOOGL)(communication services) and an overweight position in Advanced Micro Devices (AMD)(information technology).
  • Notable new positions added during the quarter included Lam Research (LRCX)(information technology), Advanced Micro Devices (information technology), and Snowflake (SNOW)(information technology). Notable sales included DoorDash (DASH)(consumer discretionary), CoStar Group (CSGP)(Real Estate), Shopify (SHOP)(information technology), and Copart (CPRT)(industrials).

Outlook

  • Whether momentum is taking a breather or we’re seeing the start of a lasting fade is not a call we are trying to make. However, we continue to see structural tailwinds and exciting multiyear trends that we believe can drive sustained growth for many businesses.
  • While we remain vigilant about macroeconomic factors, our process is driven through a bottom-up stock selection process married with top-down growth themes.
  • Our efforts, with the help of the analyst team, have consistently focused on identifying companies that can succeed in a variety of economic environments and do not require an economic acceleration to do so. We continue to monitor all information and will assess the impact to the fundamentals on a stock-by-stock basis.
  • Changes in the relative positioning of the strategy are primarily a result of our fundamental, bottom-up process of evaluating the opportunity and risk of individual stocks. Our largest absolute position is information technology; however, we are roughly in line with the benchmark in terms of our allocation. Our largest underweight is to consumer discretionary. We have no exposure to the consumer staples or energy.

data.title: ServiceNow: Enterprise Stickiness Will Not Be Threatened After AI Integration
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

ServiceNow, Inc. (NYSE: NOW) stock price has dropped over 50% over the last year driven by panic AI-related sell-off. However, I remain bullish on this company because the sentiment is an overblown one - The company has a lot of strategic moves, including the integration of Claude and OpenAI to optimize AI outcomes, that show AI is a core, functional layer within the ServiceNow ecosystem rather than a threat to its business.

From my point of view, the Company's fundamentals is resilient, given the intact financial performance that shows customer stickiness. This is in contrast of the sell-off which is triggered by worries about AI disruption and substitution. The company has managed to record a revenue growth rate YoY above 20% since 2021. At the end of FY2025, the company’s subscription revenue reached $12.9 billion. This is expected to improve to a revenue of $15.6 billion, which is a 21% YoY growth rate. The strong top-line growth is a testament to its value to its customers.

With current price returns already beginning to outperform the S&P 500, I believe this is an ideal entry point for an undervalued leader in enterprise automation. In the last month, this optimism has started manifesting in the company’s price return, which is beating the S&P 500. After the overshoot and sentiment, I believe this is where rational investors can come in.

AI Integration as a Growth Catalyst

ServiceNow. delivers cloud-based digital solutions globally. The company offers a wide range of IT services, risk, operations, workplace management, plus automation and AI-driven tools. These services serve varied industries and partners, especially those with autonomous AI. Since the company was incorporated in 2004, the Company has seen continuous growth in its customer base. Anyone in the IT industry knows, once an enterprise software is integrated into a company's system, it is hard and costly to replace it - this is where the stickiness comes in.

Let me dive in, but before that, let me first give a market picture that supports my perspective. One thing that is inseparable about cloud computing is that it is growing rapidly at a CAGR of 20.61% between 2026 and 2035. It is valued at $1.1 trillion today and is estimated to reach a market volume of $5.9 trillion by 2035.

Most of this growth is driven by task automation, which is decreasing human error, cutting costs, increasing productivity, and boosting efficiency, especially through cloud-based AI. For example, AI algorithms are working together with cloud computing to analyze and organize large data volumes to create useful blueprints. As a result, organisations are deriving value from this combination as it is offering flexible approaches, safe systems, facilitating adaptability, and enabling quick and economically strong AI systems. Therefore, AI is an augmentation to enterprise SaaS like ServiceNow by integrating advanced LLMs into its workflow, ServiceNow is enabling its customers to derive real economic value from their data and AI.

The best example is Deloitte, which has selected ServiceNow to modernize its IT service automation, reporting an 80% reduction in preparation time. Sales are benefitted by improving conversion rates, administration team is cutting time cost by almost 80%. This is a major productivity gain to Deloitte.

Some argues that with the rise of AI models, you might not need to subscribe to specific cloud computing services integrated with AI, because you can find that solution with other available AI models. While this might be true for individual users, AI systems are evolving to handle industry-specific routine tasks with precision inside a transparent and trustworthy framework. As a result, ServiceNow’s stickiness is from its ability to organize complex services into a single, trusted governance framework like Now Assist, making ServiceNow a "mission-critical" tool for organizations - this is where standalone AIs / LLMs cannot do.

In fact, when you look at the top business objectives in 2026, which are cost efficiency and workforce productivity - while risk reduction is also up in front. Therefore, when there is a service provider that can be accountable to risks and stability, companies are not willing to take this responsibility and work in house. This is exactly what ServiceNow is offering its customers with the recent AI partnerships, as long as the Company is still willing to keep up to the AI development and integration - this is the falsifiable condition to our thesis. Once ServiceNow is not considered as catching up and integrating the latest AI function integrated into the product suite, investors should review its investment into the company.

But until then, financial numbers reflect that customers are trusting the companies strongly.

Financials and Valuation

ServiceNow’s top-line performance aligns with my premises that its "stickiness" is stronger than ever. The company’s top-line trend has been on an uptrend sustainably. Since 2021, ServiceNow revenues has an over 20% YoY growth rate. Specifically, in 2025, total revenue reached $3.57 billion, while current Remaining Performance Obligations (cRPO) stand at $12.85 billion, up 25% year-over-year. This contrasts the sell-off.

Adjusted diluted EPS for Q4 is at $0.92, a 25% year-over-year increase that beat the consensus. Analysts expect FY2026 EPS to reach $4.18, which continues its double-digit growth.

When comparing valuations, ServiceNow is undervalued relative to its growth trajectory when its P/E multiple is measured against peers like Palo Alto Networks (PANW). Currently, NOW’s forward P/E ratio of 27.76x, which is lower than PANW’s 44.68x and Crowdstrike (CRWD)'s 91.03, while having comparable or even higher revenue growth.

In my view, this is an undervaluation. I believe NOW is more heavily impacted in this sell-off than its SaaS peers, which created an under-valuation opportunity. If valuation multiple converge and normalize, I think the company will have at least 20-30% higher share price when sentiment resumes, making it comparable to its top SaaS peers.

Investment Risk

  • Customer Cohort Concentration: ServiceNow has in the last 1 year has been maintaining around 98% customer renewal rate. However, I am seeing an underlying risk in over-reliance on deepening spend on existing customers as opposed to new customers. In Q3’2025, the renewal rate slipped to 97% but it has now recorded a rebound to 98% in Q4’2025. This is a testament to the fact that NOW is heavily relying on legacy customer contracts rather than new customer contracts. If the company fails to record new customers, it risks its future revenue sustainability
  • Failed to catch up to AI development: As discussed, my bullish thesis is based on the fact that ServiceNow is not being replaced by AI since it is adopting and integrating it. However, as Moore's Law goes, AI applications and ability is improving rapidly. If ServiceNow fails to follow the latest AI capability, it will be replaced.

Conclusion

I am reiterating a Buy rating on ServiceNow, as I think the sentiment during SaaS sell-off is unjustifiable and created an under-valuation opportunity in ths stock. Driven by the integration of AI-based models, ServiceNow has strong business performance and preference from its customers.

data.title: Bread Financial: Loan Growth, Single-Digit P/E, Bullish Chart - Despite Macro Risks
data.description:
Bread Financial Holdings, Inc. (BFH) has trounced its Financials-sector peers recently. Over the past year, shares have returned 58%, while the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) is higher by just 23%, and the Financials sector ETF (XLF) has returned 6%. The short-term lender has also beaten consumer stocks by a wide margin. Indeed, U.S. retail sales data has been decent over the past year, rising 6.2% in the latest Johnson Redbook retail sales report. Macro risks are apparent, of course, but I continue to like Bread.

Today, I reiterate a Buy rating. I had a Buy on BFH back in February of 2024. Shares have returned 105% since then, dividends included. That's about 70 percentage points of alpha to SPY. With a low valuation, solid growth, a new buyback, and a bullish chart, this high-risk, cyclical small-cap has legs higher, in my view.

Bread: A Quiet Financials Leader YoY

Back in January, Bread reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $2.07 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $0.48, while revenue of $975 million, up more than 5% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $20 million beat. Its income from continuing operations increased $45 million during the period, primarily driven by higher net interest income and lower provision for credit losses.

Shares jumped 7.1% in the session that followed, marking a second consecutive post-reporting climb. Looking ahead to the April update, the options market prices in a near-average 6.6% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the report. Shares of the $3.3 billion market cap Financial sector company have elevated implied volatility at 50%, while short interest is high at 9.8%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, Bread’s double beat came with impressive full-year numbers. The company tallied net income of $521 million from continuing operations for the FY 2025, with adjusted diluted EPS rising significantly to $12.16 from $7.74 in 2024.

Operational efficiency and cost discipline were particularly encouraging—Bread's adjusted total non-interest expenses decreased by 1% despite a slight decline in average loans. The firm also improved its credit quality, with its net loss rate falling to 7.7% from 8.2% the previous year.

Color on Q4

During 2025, Bread expanded its network of partners, including Cricket Wireless and Vivint, and grew direct-to-consumer deposits to $8.5 billion, which now account for 48% of total funding. What’s more, capital management remained a priority, highlighted by the repurchase of 5.7 million shares and a 10% increase in the quarterly cash dividend. Then, just last month, the company announced a $600 million increase to the buyback plan, bringing its total remaining capacity to $765 million.

On the call, the management team noted new signings with Caesar’s and Crypto.com, underscoring the upside revenue and earnings potential despite a shaky consumer backdrop. Executives also anticipate that tax refunds may positively affect consumer spending, but that was a bit of a guess, it sounded like.

Looking ahead to the current year, Bread Financial expects low-single-digit growth in both average loans and revenue, while continuing to focus on AI adoption and further improvements in net loss rates, projected to be between 7.2% and 7.4%.

FY 2026 Outlook

On the earnings outlook, despite recent volatility, Bread should maintain $10-plus operating EPS figures in the years ahead. FY 2026 may see a 10% drop, but out-year estimates are more constructive. Top line growth is steadier, in the low-mid single digits.

The sell side, meanwhile, has turned more upbeat on Bread, as evidenced by a high 10 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days, compared to just a lone downgrade. Profitability trends are also healthy right now, while credit performance improved in January.

Bread Financial: Earnings, Valuation, Revisions

On valuation, I have key updates to my previous analysis from more than two years ago. If we assume $10.40 of forward non-GAAP EPS and apply a 9x multiple, then shares should trade near $94. That leaves BFH about $20 to the cheap side today.

I assert that a high-single-digit earnings multiple is appropriate given the current mid-teens-plus EPS growth rate looking ahead to FY 2027 and 2028. Considering the cost discipline, execution prowess, and shareholder-friendly policies, a single-digit earnings multiple is conservative, in my view.

Bread: Very Low P/E and P/B

Key risks for Bread include questionable long-term loan growth potential, given rising competition and emerging credit quality pressure. Investors should monitor monthly credit card delinquency data for clues on how Bread’s book may materialize in the future.

Of course, a macroeconomic downturn would be sharply negative for a cyclical and consumer company like Bread. Loss of key retail partners would be another major headwind if we begin to see that, along with adverse regulatory changes.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q1 2026 earnings date of Thursday, April 23 BMO. Shares pay a $0.23 quarterly dividend on Monday, March 16.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares significantly undervalued, BFH’s technical situation is bullish. Notice in the chart below that the stock is currently in a symmetrical triangle, with a potential apex coming about near $75. Investors should monitor the pattern, but the presumption is that BFH will break out in the trend of larger degree (higher). What’s more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is likewise coiling—a breakout or breakdown there could be a telltale sign of what price does.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It's on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Now, with key long-term support near $68 and a high amount of volume by price below that, there should be ample cushion if we see another 10% pullback.

In terms of upside potential, the breakout above $68 triggered an upside measured move price objective to $98, based on the depth of the late 2024 to April decline and subsequent breakout.

BFH: Bullish Consolidation After Last Year's Breakout, $98 Target

The Bottom Line

I have a Buy rating on Bread. This small-cap micro lender sports a low P/E valuation, strong technicals, and a proven management team. Yes, the macro risks are many, but price action and fundamental growth appear solid.
